rationale: The subject roof at 3561 E 102nd Ct, Thornton, CO displays a hip/gable hybrid with complex intersecting valleys creating the characteristic "4"-shaped plan pattern shared uniformly across all adjacent attached units. Ground shadows cast from the eave extend approximately 35–45% of the building's half-width, consistent with a moderate 6:12 pitch. The ridge is clearly inset from the eave footprint but not dramatically so, and the visible roof-plane surface area from directly overhead is moderate — neither the broad exposure of a shallow 4:12 nor the compressed appearance of a steep 8:12+. Colorado's northern climate and snow-load requirements, combined with the builder-grade townhome construction style typical of the Denver metro in the 2000s–2010s, strongly support a standard 6:12 pitch. Confidence is moderate (0.72) due to the near-nadir viewing angle limiting precise shadow triangulation.
